task: I’m getting ready to take my car in for a routine tire check, and I want to make sure I’m not overlooking anything basic before I hand it over. What should I verify on the paperwork and on the car itself so I can catch any mismatch early?

turns.agent_response:
Here’s a tight pre-dropoff checklist so you can catch mismatches early.

### On the paperwork
Verify these against the tires currently on the car and the door placard:

- **Tire size**: exact size on the sidewall matches what’s on the work order  
  - Example: `225/45R17`
- **Load index and speed rating**: should be acceptable for your vehicle and match or exceed spec
  - Example: `94V`
- **Brand/model**: if they’re replacing or rotating, confirm what’s actually listed
- **Quantity and placement**:
  - same spec all around, or
  - mixed front/rear if your car uses staggered fitment
- **Axle position**: if directional or staggered tires are involved, confirm they’re assigned to the correct axle
- **Pressure recorded**:
  - compare listed PSI to the **door placard**
  - check whether the shop notes **front/rear split** if your car uses different pressures
- **Cold vs hot pressure**: note whether PSI was taken **cold** or **after a road test**
- **TPMS service**: look for a note that TPMS was **reset/relearned** if applicable
- **Torque spec**: confirm wheel torque value is listed, if they provide it
- **Old tire disposition**: if relevant, note whether old tires were returned, recycled, or kept

### On the car itself
Before you hand it over, quickly check:

- **All four tires’ sidewalls** for:
  - size
  - load index
  - speed rating
  - direction arrow if directional
- **Tread and wear pattern**:
  - even wear or unusual shoulder wear
  - any bulges, cuts, or sidewall damage
- **Valve stems / TPMS sensors**:
  - caps present
  - no visible damage
- **Wheel condition**:
  - no bent rim, cracks, or missing lug nuts
- **Door jamb placard**:
  - confirm recommended PSI
  - note whether front/rear pressures differ
- **If you have staggered or directional tires**:
  - confirm the tires are on the correct axle and facing the correct direction

### Quick “don’t miss this” items
If you only check five things, make them:

1. **Size matches sidewall**
2. **Load index/speed rating are correct**
3. **PSI matches door placard**
4. **TPMS reset/relearn is noted**
5. **Directional/staggered tires are on the correct axle**

If you want, I can turn this into a **one-page checklist** you can keep on your phone and use at the shop.

user_simulator.text:
Pretty solid. But I’d want one more layer on the paperwork side: actual **measured tread depth** and **which tires were moved where**. Shops love writing “rotation completed” and leaving it vague.

What I’d verify before and after:

- **Tire size** on the work order vs sidewall
- **Load index / speed rating** exactly or better
- **DOT date code** if they’re putting on a tire that’s not brand new
- **Measured tread depth** on each tire, not just “good”
- **Rotation pattern**: LF/RF/RR/LR listed clearly
- **PSI**: cold pressure, and whether they set front/rear differently
- **TPMS**: reset/relearn noted if the car needs it
- **Torque spec** and whether lug nuts were rechecked after lowering
- **Any existing damage**: nail, bulge, curb rash, bent rim, uneven wear

On the car itself, I’d check:

- sidewall size/load/speed rating
- tread depth and uneven wear
- valve stems and TPMS caps
- wheel damage / missing lug nuts
- placard pressure in the door jamb
- if directional, arrow points the right way

And honestly, I’d take a quick photo of each tire before I hand it over. Last time I had a tire shop get sloppy, they wrote down the wrong pressure rating on the invoice — had to go back and make them fix it. Photos make that conversation a lot shorter.

If you want, I can tighten this into a simple 10-point inspection sheet, no extra fluff.

turns.agent_response:
Yep — that’s the right extra layer. The two things that most often get hand-waved are **measured tread depth** and **exact tire position changes**.

Here’s the tightened version I’d use:

### Paperwork: verify these explicitly
- **Tire size** matches sidewall and work order
- **Load index / speed rating** are correct
- **DOT date code** if any tire is not new
- **Measured tread depth** for each tire
- **Rotation pattern / tire positions**
  - list which tire moved from **LF / RF / LR / RR** to where
- **PSI setting**
  - note **cold pressure**
  - note if front/rear are different
- **TPMS reset/relearn** if applicable
- **Torque spec**
  - and whether lug torque was rechecked after lowering
- **Existing damage noted**
  - nail, bulge, curb rash, bent rim, uneven wear

### On the car: check before handing it over
- sidewall **size / load / speed rating**
- **tread depth** and uneven wear
- **valve stems / TPMS caps**
- **wheel damage** and missing lug nuts
- **door jamb placard pressure**
- **directional arrow** if applicable

### Best quick habit
Take **photos of all four tires** before drop-off:
- sidewall
- tread
- any damage
- wheel position

That gives you a clean reference if the invoice says something vague like “rotation completed” or the pressures don’t line up.

If you want, I can turn this into a **plain 1-page shop checklist** you can save on your phone.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, that’s the level I wanted. Clean and specific.

The only thing I’d maybe add for my own use is mileage on the paperwork — just so if they’re trying to say tread wear is from rotation interval or whatever, I’ve got a timestamp. But what you gave is solid.

The photo habit is right too. I’ve learned the same way: vague invoice language is where the mess starts.

So yeah, this works.
